name: Acute tadalafil did not substantially improve Wingate anaerobic performance
statement: In 12 well-trained subjects, tadalafil 20 mg did not substantially influence Wingate anaerobic performance indices versus placebo; only 3-minute recovery lactate increased and time to peak power decreased.
population_or_context: Well-trained subjects performing a 30-second Wingate anaerobic power test.
quantitative_note: Three-minute recovery lactate was higher with tadalafil (13.9 +/- 1.7 vs 12.8 +/- 1.3 mmol/L; P<0.05), time to peak power decreased (6.3 +/- 1.3 vs 5.7 +/- 1.5 s; P=0.05), and other parameters did not differ.

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase_note: Abstract reports methods, Wingate outcomes, lactate, time-to-peak-power, and conclusion.

caveats: No chronic training or strength endpoint.
population_profile: Healthy trained subjects; relevant to gym-performance claims but not broad physical function.
tadalafil_exposure: Single tadalafil 20 mg dose the day before Wingate testing.
endpoint_measure: Wingate mean power, peak power, time to peak power, fatigue index, and blood lactate recovery.
effect_estimate: No differences in most anaerobic performance parameters; lactate at 3 minutes and time to peak power changed.
safety_findings: No adverse-event rates reported in abstract; lactate recovery changed.
directness_to_target_population: Moderate for acute anaerobic performance in trained men; low for chronic daily tadalafil or hypertrophy claims.
confounding_and_bias_limits: Small sample, acute exposure, limited outcomes, and abstract-level extraction.
class_transfer_limits: Direct tadalafil evidence but narrow to acute anaerobic testing.
